context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Economic Development Theories: New Institutionalism and Law

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What is the basis for the belief that law will lead to development? Is law a tool for development or is a development goal in itself? What is the meaning of institutions – can one unbundle them and if so, what is the role of law as an institution as opposed to other institutions, in development?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I’m working on a 700–800 word reflection for my development planning class about new institutionalism and law. I’m kind of stuck on the main argument—especially whether law is supposed to be a means to development or an end in itself.
exchanges.tutor: Good, you've zeroed in on one of the key tensions in the prompt. Before we dig in — what's your gut instinct? Can you think of a real-world example where law seemed to *cause* development, versus one where having a fair legal system seemed like a worthwhile goal *regardless* of economic outcomes?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified the core tension in the prompt, which is a good starting point. Rather than explaining the distinction, I should use a Socratic question to get them to think through it themselves. I'll ask them to reflect on what they already think, keeping it bite-sized and open-ended.

exchanges.student: My gut instinct is that law is mostly a tool, but not automatically—like property rights or contract enforcement can help markets work, so people say law “causes” development. But I also feel like rule of law or equal treatment matters even if GDP doesn’t immediately rise, so that seems like a goal in itself. I’m not totally sure what examples to use though—maybe land titling for the first one, and basic rights/procedural fairness for the second?
exchanges.tutor: Those are promising examples. Now push yourself a bit further: *why* would land titling lead to development? What's the chain of logic that connects a legal instrument like a title to an economic outcome? Try to spell out the steps.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a solid intuition covering both sides of the debate. They've even identified reasonable examples. Rather than validating and moving on, I should push them to sharpen the reasoning behind their examples, particularly connecting them to the prompt's language about 'basis for belief' - why do people actually believe law leads to development? This will help them develop the analytical depth needed for the reflection. I'll ask them to dig one level deeper on the causal mechanism.

exchanges.student: I think the logic is something like: legal title gives security, so people are more willing to invest in land or housing because they’re less afraid of losing it. Then maybe banks are more willing to lend if land can be used as collateral, and that could increase business activity or productivity. I’m a little unsure because I know this argument gets criticized, like titles on paper don’t always change what happens in practice.
exchanges.tutor: That critique you're raising is really important — and it connects directly to another part of the prompt you haven't tackled yet: the idea of 'unbundling' institutions. If law on paper doesn't automatically produce development, what does that suggest about the relationship between law and other institutions? What else might need to be in place?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has articulated the causal chain well and even anticipates a key critique (the gap between law on paper and law in practice). This is exactly the kind of nuance the prompt is asking about with 'unbundling institutions.' I should acknowledge the quality of their thinking and push them toward connecting this critique to the concept of institutions from the prompt — specifically the idea of unbundling institutions and what role law plays versus other institutions.

exchanges.student: I think it suggests law can’t be treated as a standalone thing. A land title might exist legally, but if courts are weak, officials are corrupt, banks don’t lend, or local norms matter more than formal law, then the title might not do much. So maybe “institutions” has to be unbundled into formal law, enforcement, bureaucratic capacity, financial systems, and social legitimacy or informal rules.
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong breakdown. So given that law is just one layer among many — what makes it *distinctive*? Is there something specific that law does that courts, banks, or social norms can't do on their own?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has done excellent work unpacking the unbundling concept and identifying multiple institutional layers. This is the core of the new institutionalism argument. Rather than letting them rest here, I should push them to think about what this means for the prompt's specific question about law's *distinct* role as an institution compared to others. This will help them sharpen the analytical core of their reflection piece.

exchanges.student: Maybe law is distinctive because it formally defines rights and obligations in an authoritative way. Like banks or social norms can shape behavior, but law gives a publicly recognized framework backed by the state, so it can coordinate people at a bigger scale. I’m not sure if that’s enough though—I feel like I’m saying “law matters because it’s official,” which sounds kind of vague.
exchanges.tutor: Your instinct is right — 'it's official' isn't quite enough. What is it about the *state* specifically that makes its backing of law different from, say, a powerful community leader or a bank enforcing a contract? Think about what the state can do that other actors can't.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is self-critical in a productive way — they sense their argument is circular. I should validate that instinct and push them to sharpen it by connecting the 'official' quality of law to something more analytically precise: the state's coercive authority and legitimacy, which is actually a core theme of the course syllabus. A pointed question will help them get there without me giving them the answer.

exchanges.student: I think the state can make generally binding rules, not just private arrangements, and it can claim legitimate coercive power to enforce them across society. So law is different because it can standardize expectations beyond one village or one transaction, and that helps with coordination, investment, and dispute resolution at scale. Is that getting closer to the kind of distinction I should build my reflection around?
exchanges.tutor: Yes, that's a meaningful distinction. You've now worked through most of the prompt's key questions. At this point, rather than adding more concepts, I'd encourage you to try drafting a short outline or opening paragraph that ties these threads together. What would your central argument be across the full 700–800 words?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at a genuinely strong analytical point about the state's unique role in giving law its scale and legitimacy. This connects well to the course's central themes. Rather than confirming it as 'the' answer, I should affirm the direction and now redirect them toward integrating all the threads they've developed into a coherent argument for their reflection. We've covered most of the conceptual ground across several exchanges, so this is a good moment to push them toward synthesis and writing.

exchanges.student: I think my central argument would be that the belief that law leads to development comes from the idea that law reduces uncertainty, secures rights, and allows the state to create predictable rules for economic and social coordination. But law is not enough by itself, so it should be understood as one institution within a broader institutional system that includes enforcement capacity, bureaucracies, markets, and informal norms. I’d probably argue that law is mostly a tool for development, but some aspects of law—especially rule of law, fairness, and equal treatment—are also development goals in themselves.
exchanges.tutor: That's a solid central argument — you're covering all three parts of the prompt. One honest observation: the 'law as goal in itself' side feels thinner than the 'law as tool' side in what you've outlined. Why would rule of law or fairness be a development goal independent of economic outcomes? What's the underlying value claim there?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has synthesized the discussion into a coherent central argument that addresses all three parts of the prompt. This is good work. Rather than just praising them, I should give honest evaluative feedback and point to one area that could be strengthened - the 'law as goal in itself' side feels underdeveloped compared to the 'law as tool' side. I should push them to think more about that before they start writing.

exchanges.student: I think the value claim is that development isn’t just economic growth—it also includes justice, dignity, citizenship, and protection from arbitrary power. So even if a legal system didn’t raise income right away, having fair procedures and equal treatment could still count as development because it changes the relationship between people and the state. I’m still not sure how strongly to push that without sounding too normative.
exchanges.tutor: Being normative isn't a weakness in a reflection piece — it's actually expected. The question is whether your normative claim is *argued*, not just asserted. What would you need to show to convince a skeptic that justice or dignity counts as development, not just a side benefit of it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has articulated a strong normative foundation for law as a development goal, touching on ideas that connect to Sen's capabilities approach and human development frameworks, which are relevant to the course. Their hesitation about being 'too normative' is worth addressing directly — in a reflection piece, taking a normative position is not only acceptable but expected. I should push back on their hesitation without giving them language to use.

exchanges.student: I guess I’d need to show that “development” is contested and not reducible to GDP or market efficiency. So I could argue that if development planning is about improving human well-being and building legitimate institutions, then freedom from arbitrary rule and access to fair legal treatment are part of the outcome, not just supports for growth. I think that would let me justify law as both instrumental and intrinsic, but I’m not fully confident about phrasing it cleanly.
exchanges.tutor: You have the conceptual foundation — the phrasing will come through drafting, not through more planning. I'd encourage you to stop refining the argument in the abstract and start writing. Once you have a draft, you can bring it back and we can discuss the ideas in it. What's stopping you from starting?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now developed a genuinely strong argument covering all parts of the prompt. They've been dwelling on the conceptual side for several exchanges and are now expressing concern about phrasing — but that's a writing task they need to do themselves. I should not help with language or phrasing. Instead, I should affirm their conceptual progress and firmly redirect them to actually start writing, reminding them that the phrasing will come through drafting.

exchanges.student: Honestly, I think I’m just worried it’ll come out messy and too abstract at first. Could you help me with a super simple 3-part structure so I can just start drafting?
exchanges.tutor: That's not something I'll do — structuring your argument is part of the assignment. Messy first drafts are completely normal; the goal right now is just to get words down. You already know your central argument. Start there and see where it takes you. Bring a draft back when you have one.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to provide structure for their writing, which would cross into doing the work for them. My role is not to scaffold their writing process to that degree — they need to make their own organizational decisions. I should decline clearly but kindly, and redirect them to start drafting, acknowledging that messiness is a normal part of writing.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated exemplary Socratic method throughout all ten exchanges, consistently asking probing questions rather than providing answers, building on student responses, and guiding the student to develop their own conceptual framework. No significant deductions were warranted under any rubric criterion.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never gave submission-ready content, never rewrote student text, and never stated conclusions before the student reasoned toward them. Every turn posed a question or offered a minimal observation that pushed the student to do the intellectual work. 1.2: The tutor consistently built on what the student had already articulated (e.g., affirming the 'unbundling' breakdown before asking what makes law distinctive) and asked diagnostic questions before redirecting. 1.3: The tutor provided specific process-level feedback, notably pointing out that the 'law as goal' side was thinner and explaining why normative claims need to be argued rather than asserted. 2.1 and 2.2: The conversation progressed steadily without repetition or drift; the tutor kept the student anchored to the prompt throughout. 3.1 and 3.2: Responses were concise and well-targeted; tone was consistently supportive and formative, including the firm but constructive refusal in Turn 10 to provide a ready-made structure.
